subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Function Analysis with Accumulation
stimulus:
The graph of the differentiable function $f$, shown for $-6 \leq x \leq 7$, has a horizontal tangent at $x = -2$ and is linear for $0 \leq x \leq 7$. Let $R$ be the region in the second quadrant bounded by the graph of $f$, the vertical line $x = -6$, and the $x$- and $y$-axes. Region $R$ has area 12.

Selected values of $f$ are given: $f(0) = 0$, $f(4) = 6$, $f(6) = 9$, and the line through $(0,0)$ and $(7,10.5)$ has slope $\dfrac{3}{2}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 7$. The area of region $R$ (between the graph and the axes in the second quadrant, from $x = -6$ to $x = 0$) is 12.

parts.prompt: The function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = \displaystyle\int_0^x f(t)\,dt$. Find the values of $g(-6)$, $g(4)$, and $g(6)$.

scoring_points.description: $g(-6) = \displaystyle\int_0^{-6} f(t)\,dt = -\displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f(t)\,dt$. Since region $R$ has area 12 and lies below the $x$-axis in the second quadrant (where $f < 0$), $\displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f(t)\,dt = -12$, so $g(-6) = -(-12) = 12$... Alternatively: $f \leq 0$ on $[-6,0]$ (region $R$ is below $x$-axis), so $\displaystyle\int_{-6}^{0} f(t)\,dt = -12$, giving $g(-6) = -(-12) = 12$. Note: the exact sign depends on whether $f$ is above or below the $x$-axis in $[-6,0]$. Since $R$ is bounded by the graph, vertical line $x=-6$, and the axes, $f \leq 0$ on $(-6,0)$, so $g(-6) = 12$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g(-6) = 12$ (recognizes $\int_{-6}^0 f\,dt = -12$ because $f\leq 0$ on $[-6,0]$ and area of $R$ is $12$)

sample_responses.response: $g(-6) = \displaystyle\int_0^{-6} f(t)\,dt = -\displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f(t)\,dt$. Since $f \leq 0$ on $[-6,0]$, $\displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f(t)\,dt = -12$. Therefore $g(-6) = -(-12) = 12$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly reverses the limits (picking up a negative sign), then uses the fact that $f\leq 0$ on $[-6,0]$ so the signed integral equals $-12$.
Forgets the sign reversal from flipping the limits. $g(-6) = \int_0^{-6} f = -\int_{-6}^0 f = -(-12) = 12$, not $-12$.

scoring_points.description: For $0 \leq x \leq 7$, $f$ is linear with $f(0) = 0$ and slope $\dfrac{3}{2}$, so $f(t) = \dfrac{3}{2}t$. Thus $g(4) = \displaystyle\int_0^4 \dfrac{3}{2}t\,dt = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{t^2}{2}\Big|_0^4 = \dfrac{3}{4}(16) = 12$.

sample_responses.response: Since $f$ is linear on $[0,7]$ with $f(0)=0$ and slope $\frac{3}{2}$, $g(4) = \displaystyle\int_0^4 \frac{3}{2}t\,dt = \frac{3}{4}t^2\Big|_0^4 = \frac{3}{4}(16) = 12$.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the geometric interpretation or direct integration of the linear $f(t) = \frac{3}{2}t$ to obtain $g(4) = 12$.
Confuses $g(4) = \int_0^4 f(t)\,dt$ with $f(4)$. The accumulation function $g$ integrates $f$; it does not evaluate $f$ at the upper limit.
Correctly integrates $f(t) = \frac{3}{2}t$ from $0$ to $6$, or equivalently computes the triangular area $\frac{1}{2}(6)(9) = 27$.

parts.prompt: For the function $g$ defined in part (a), find all values of $x$ in the interval $0 \leq x \leq 6$ at which the graph of $g$ has a critical point. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f(x)$. A critical point of $g$ occurs where $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: by the Fundamental Theorem, $g'(x) = f(x)$; critical points occur where $f(x)=0$

sample_responses.response: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f(x)$. Critical points of $g$ occur where $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly invokes FTC Part 1 to identify $g' = f$, then notes that critical points occur at zeros of $f$.
Confuses $g'(x) = f(x)$ (from FTC) with $g'(x) = f'(x)$. Since $g(x) = \int_0^x f(t)\,dt$, FTC Part 1 gives $g'(x) = f(x)$, not $f'(x)$.

required_elements: $g'(x) = f(x)$ by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

scoring_points.description: Since $f(x) = \dfrac{3}{2}x$ for $0 \leq x \leq 6$, $f(x) = 0$ only at $x = 0$. Thus $g$ has a critical point at $x = 0$. Because $f(x) > 0$ for $x > 0$, $g'(x) > 0$ for $x \in (0, 6]$, so $x = 0$ is not a local minimum or maximum in the interior; $x = 0$ is a critical point (endpoint where $g' = 0$).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $x=0$ is the only critical point of $g$ on $[0,6]$ because $f(x)=\frac{3}{2}x=0$ only at $x=0$ on this interval

sample_responses.response: Since $f(x) = \frac{3}{2}x$ on $[0,6]$, $f(x) = 0$ only at $x=0$. So $x=0$ is the only critical point of $g$ on $[0,6]$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that the linear function $f(x) = \frac{3}{2}x$ equals zero only at the origin on the interval $[0,6]$.

sample_responses.response: $g$ has no critical points on $[0,6]$ since $f(x) > 0$ for all $x > 0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Overlooks $x=0$, where $f(0) = 0$, giving $g'(0) = 0$. The endpoint $x=0$ is a critical point of $g$ because $g'(0)=0$.

required_elements: $x = 0$ is the only critical point in $[0, 6]$

common_traps: Saying there are no critical points and forgetting $x = 0$
Not using the Fundamental Theorem to connect $g'$ and $f$

parts.prompt: The function $h$ is defined by $h(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-6}^x f'(t)\,dt$. Find the values of $h(6)$, $h'(6)$, and $h''(6)$. Show the work that leads to your answers.

scoring_points.description: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $h(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-6}^x f'(t)\,dt = f(x) - f(-6)$. Thus $h(6) = f(6) - f(-6)$. Using $f(6) = \dfrac{3}{2}(6) = 9$ (from the linear piece on $[0,7]$) and reading $f(-6)$ from the graph. The numerical value of $h(6)$ depends on $f(-6)$ as read from the provided graph.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $h(x) = f(x)-f(-6)$ by FTC, so $h(6) = f(6)-f(-6)$ with $f(6)=9$ from the linear piece

sample_responses.response: By the Fundamental Theorem: $h(x) = \displaystyle\int_{-6}^x f'(t)\,dt = f(x)-f(-6)$. Thus $h(6) = f(6)-f(-6) = 9-f(-6)$.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies FTC correctly: $\int_{-6}^x f'(t)\,dt = f(x)-f(-6)$. Uses $f(6) = \frac{3}{2}(6)=9$ from the linear formula.
Confuses the integral of $f'$ with the value of $f'$. By FTC, $\int_{-6}^6 f'(t)\,dt = f(6)-f(-6)$, which is a net change in $f$, not the value of the derivative.

scoring_points.description: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $h'(x) = f'(x)$. For $0 \leq x \leq 7$, $f$ is linear with slope $\dfrac{3}{2}$, so $f'(6) = \dfrac{3}{2}$. Thus $h'(6) = \dfrac{3}{2}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $h'(6) = f'(6) = \dfrac{3}{2}$ (since $f$ is linear with slope $\frac{3}{2}$ on $[0,7]$)

sample_responses.response: By FTC, $h'(x) = f'(x)$. Since $f$ is linear on $[0,7]$ with slope $\frac{3}{2}$, $f'(6) = \frac{3}{2}$, so $h'(6) = \frac{3}{2}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Differentiates $h$ using FTC Part 1, then reads the slope of the linear piece of $f$.
Uses an average rate of change instead of $h'(6) = f'(6)$. FTC immediately gives $h'(x) = f'(x)$, so $h'(6) = f'(6) = 3/2$.

scoring_points.description: $h''(x) = f''(x)$. Since $f$ is linear on $0 \leq x \leq 7$, $f''(x) = 0$ for $0 < x < 7$. Therefore $h''(6) = f''(6) = 0$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $h''(6) = f''(6) = 0$ because $f$ is linear on $[0,7]$

sample_responses.commentary: Uses that a linear function has zero second derivative.
Confuses $f'$ (slope of $f$) with $f''$ (concavity of $f$). Since $f$ is linear, $f' = \frac{3}{2}$ is constant, so $f'' = 0$.

scoring_points.description: All three values $h(6)$, $h'(6)$, $h''(6)$ computed correctly with supporting work shown.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: all three values $h(6)$, $h'(6)$, $h''(6)$ correct with work shown

sample_responses.commentary: Provides all three values with clear supporting calculations.
Omits $h(6)$. All three values are required with supporting work to earn this point.

required_elements: All three values correct with work shown

common_traps: Applying the Fundamental Theorem incorrectly (confusing $h = \int f'$ with $h = f$)
